questions.text: Frogs differ from humans because they possess:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Both frogs and humans have paired cerebral hemispheres in their brains. The cerebral hemispheres are the largest part of the brain and are divided into left and right hemispheres.

Both frogs and humans have a hepatic portal system. The hepatic portal system is a network of blood vessels that directs blood from the digestive organs, such as the stomach and intestines, to the liver before it returns to the heart.
Human erythrocytes are enucleated and discoidal while in frogs erythrocytes are large, oval and biconvex nucleated cells. Erythrocytes are the carriers of haemoglobin.

options.text: A thyroid as well as parathyroid
options.explanation: Both frogs and humans have a thyroid gland and parathyroid glands.

questions.explanation: Human erythrocytes are enucleated and discoidal while in frogs erythrocytes are large, oval and biconvex nucleated cells. Erythrocytes are the carriers of haemoglobin.

questions.text: Which one of the following structures in Pheretima is correctly matched with its function?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In a mature earthworm, the 14th and 16th segments are covered by a prominent dark band of glandular tissues called clitellum which secretes cocoon where fertilization and development takes place. In alimentary canal,the muscular gizzard (8th and 9th segments) helps in grinding the soil particles and decaying leaves. The characteristic feature of the intestine between 26th and 35th segments is the presence of an internal median fold of dorsal wall called typhlosole. This increases the effective area of absorption in the intestine. In each body segment, except the first, last and clitellum, there are rows of S shaped setae, embedded in the epidermal pits in the middle of each segment. Setae can be extended or retracted. Their principal role is in locomotion.

The gizzard in earthworms (such as Pheretima) is not involved in the absorption of digested food. The gizzard is a muscular structure located in the digestive system, and its main function is mechanical digestion.

Setae, which are bristle-like structures on the body of earthworms like Pheretima, are primarily involved in locomotion and burrowing rather than defense against predators. Earthworms use their setae to anchor themselves in the soil and facilitate movement through peristaltic contractions.

The typhlosole is not primarily involved in the storage of extra nutrients in earthworms, including Pheretima. The typhlosole is a structural fold in the intestine of certain earthworms, and its main function is to increase the surface area for absorption of nutrients.

questions.explanation: In a mature earthworm, the 14th and 16th segments are covered by a prominent dark band of glandular tissues called clitellum which secretes cocoon where fertilization and development takes place. In alimentary canal,the muscular gizzard (8th and 9th segments) helps in grinding the soil particles and decaying leaves. The characteristic feature of the intestine between 26th and 35th segments is the presence of an internal median fold of dorsal wall called typhlosole. This increases the effective area of absorption in the intestine. In each body segment, except the first, last and clitellum, there are rows of S shaped setae, embedded in the epidermal pits in the middle of each segment. Setae can be extended or retracted. Their principal role is in locomotion.

questions.text: Which one of the following correctly describes the location of some body parts in the earthworm Pheretima?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Four pairs of spermathecae in the 4th and 7th segments
options.explanation: The most common arrangement is two pairs of spermathecae in segments 4 and 5.

options.text: One pair of ovaries attached at the intersegmental septum of the 14th and 15th segments
options.explanation: Earthworms typically have a pair of ovaries, but they are located in the 12th and 13th segments, not the 14th and 15th segments.

options.text: Two pairs of testes in the 10th and 11th segments
options.explanation: In the Pheretima, two pairs of the testis sac are situated in the tenth and eleventh segments. Each testis sac of the tenth segment encloses a testis and a seminal funnel. Each testis sac of the eleventh segment encloses a testis, a seminal vesicle and a seminal funnel.

options.text: Two pairs of accessory glands in the 16th and 18th segments
options.explanation: Earthworms typically have four pairs of accessory glands located in segments 14, 15, 17, and 19. These glands play a role in producing the mucous and cocoon materials during reproduction. The 16th and 18th segments are not typically associated with the presence of accessory glands in the reproductive system of earthworms.

questions.explanation: In the Pheretima, two pairs of the testis sac are situated in the tenth and eleventh segments. Each testis sac of the tenth segment encloses a testis and a seminal funnel. Each testis sac of the eleventh segment encloses a testis, a seminal vesicle and a seminal funnel.

questions.text: Earthworms have no skeleton but during burrowing, the anterior end becomes turgid and acts as a hydraulic skeleton.This is due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect.
This is incorrect.

The hydraulic skeleton is the system of support found in soft-bodied invertebrates which relies on the incompressibility of fluids contained within the body cavity. In earthworms the coelomic fluid is under pressure within the coelom and therefore provides support for internal organs. Due to the hydraulic skeleton, during burrowing, the anterior end becomes turgid and aids in the relaxation of longitudinal muscles.
This is incorrect.

options.text: Cockroach: 10 pairs of spiracles(2 pairs on thorax and 8 pairs on abdomen)
options.explanation: There are 10 pairs of spiracles in cockroach. Two pairs are thoracic in which first pair is known as mesothoracic, lying infront of the mesothorax between the bases of first and second pair of legs and are the largest. The second pair is called metathoracic. Abdominal spiracles are eight pairs. The first pair is dorsal in position and lies on the lateral margins of the first abdominal tergum. The remaining are situated on the sides of their corresponding segments on the pleura between the terga and sterna. Spiracles are meant for intake of fresh air and release of foul air.

questions.text: The blood of Pheretima is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The circulatory or blood vascular system of earthworm is a closed system consisting of blood vessels and capillaries which ramify all parts of the body. Blood is composed of fluid plasma and colourless corpuscles, physiologically comparable to the leucocytes of the vertebrates. The red respiratory pigment, haemoglobin (or erythrocruorin) occurs dissolved in plasma. It gives a red colour to blood and aids in the transportation of oxygen for respiration.

questions.text: Pheretima posthuma are highly useful because:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Their burrows make the soil loose
They make the soil porous, leave their castings and take organic debris in the soil
options.explanation: Pheretima Posthuma are highly useful and beneficial in agriculture. Their habit of burrowing and swallowing earth makes it porous and increases the soil fertility in many ways. Their burrows permit penetration of air and moisture in porous soil and their excretory wastes and other secretions also enrich soil by adding nitrogenous matters to the soil. Pheretima posthuma is not used as fish meal. Whereas a small white earthworm (Enchytraeus albidus) is often grown in soil and used to feed aquarium fish.

questions.text: Photoreceptors of the earthworm occur on their:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Photoreceptors are restricted only to the dorsal surface. They are more numerous on the prostomium and peristomium of the earthworm and gradually reduce in number towards the posterior end of the body. They are totally absent in the clitellum. Each photoreceptor consists of a single ovoid cell, with a nucleus and clear cytoplasm containing a network of neurofibrillae and a small transparent L shaped lens or optic organelle made up of a hyaline substance. Photoreceptors enable worms to judge the intensity and duration of light.

questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The visceral mesoderm is a layer of the mesoderm in embryonic development that surrounds and supports the internal organs or viscera, including the heart, lungs, and digestive organs. It plays a vital role in the formation and development of these organs during embryogenesis, providing structural and functional support to ensure proper organ function.

The parietal mesoderm is another layer of the mesoderm that forms during early embryonic development. It lines the body cavity walls and is involved in creating the connective tissues, bones, and muscles of the limbs and trunk, contributing to the overall body structure.

The splanchnic mesoderm is a layer of the mesoderm that develops during embryogenesis. It surrounds the endoderm-derived organs, such as the heart, blood vessels, and portions of the digestive and respiratory systems, playing a crucial role in their formation and support.

The endoderm is one of the three primary germ layers in early embryonic development, giving rise to the innermost tissue layer in animals. It forms the lining of the digestive tract and respiratory system and contributes to various internal organs like the liver, pancreas, and lungs.

questions.explanation: The visceral mesoderm is a layer of the mesoderm in embryonic development that surrounds and supports the internal organs or viscera, including the heart, lungs, and digestive organs. It plays a vital role in the formation and development of these organs during embryogenesis, providing structural and functional support to ensure proper organ function.

questions.text: _is a human parasite commonly known as pinworm.

options.explanation: Locomotary setae are for crawling and are the bristles that are visible on the exterior of the earthworm. Slow creeping movements of Neries are carried out by the action of parapodia only.
The organs of locomotion in annelids are chitinous chaetae or setae embedded in sacs (earthworms) or on. parapodia present in the body wall (e.g., Nereis). Chaetae are absent in Leech.
Locomotary setae are for crawling and are the bristles that are visible on the exterior of the earthworm. Slow creeping movements of Neries are carried out by the action of parapodia only.
Slow creeping movements of Neries are carried out by the action of parapodia only.

questions.explanation: Locomotary setae are for crawling and are the bristles that are visible on the exterior of the earthworm. Slow creeping movements of Neries are carried out by the action of parapodia only.

questions.text: The most successful group and the largest phylum of the animals:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A diverse group including snails, clams, and octopuses; is not the largest phylum, but still significant in terms of species diversity.
Comprises sponges; not the largest phylum, limited in species diversity compared to others.
Contains segmented worms like earthworms and leeches, diverse but not the largest phylum.
Phylum Arthropoda is a large group consisting of a great variety among them.

questions.explanation: More than 50% of total biodiversity of our planet is formed by the Arthropods.

questions.text: Natural silk is not:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Natural silk is an example of Fibrous proteins.
Silk is produced by specialized glands in insects, like silkworms, but they're not typically considered modified salivary glands.
The useful insect is the honey bee that provides man with honey and also wax. Similarly, the silkworm gives us silk.
This option is incorrect. Natural silk is a protein in nature and is produced by silkworms.

questions.explanation: Natural silk is fibrous protein produced by larvae of silk worm.

questions.text: Scavenger insects may eat:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Dead matter present in an organism
options.explanation: This can include dead cells or tissues within a living organism, but scavenger insects typically feed on external dead material.
Some insects are scavengers and they eat up dead animal and vegetable matter. Insect larvae are a source of food for fish.
While some insects feed exclusively on dead plant matter, scavenger insects aren't restricted to only plant sources.
Scavenger insects scavenge and feed on dead, decaying material rather than preying on live animals.

questions.explanation: Scavengers are those animals that feed on dead animals and plants e.g. Drosophila and Vulture.

questions.text: Members of this phylum are exclusively marine:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phylum containing sponges, which can be found in both marine and freshwater environments.
Includes jellyfish, corals, and sea anemones, most of which are marine, but some can also be found in freshwater.
This phylum encompasses diverse creatures like snails, clams, and octopuses, found in marine, freshwater, and terrestrial habitats.
There are over 5,000 known species of echinoderms. They are marine organisms living at the sea bottom.

questions.explanation: The echinoderms are exclusively marine.

questions.text: The organs of locomotion in echinoderms are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This isn't a specific locomotion organ in echinoderms; they primarily use other structures for movement.
Typically seen in animals like squids and certain aquatic invertebrates, not a method used by echinoderms.
Found in certain segmented worms, not in echinoderms; these structures aid in movement but are not present in this phylum.
The motile species move with the help of tube feet. Each tube foot is a soft saclike structure present along the edges of grooves present in the arms.

questions.explanation: Organs of locomotion in echinoderms are tube feet. These are present along the edges of grooves present in the arms.

questions.text: Water enters the water vascular system through:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Part of the water vascular system in echinoderms, but not the entry point for water into the system.
These are bulb-like structures connected to the tube feet and are involved in controlling their extension and retraction but not the entry point for water.
Openings in the heart of arthropods are not relevant to the water vascular system in echinoderms.
The water circulates through these channels. Water enters these canals through a sieve-like plate called madreporite present on the aboral body surface.

questions.explanation: The explanation is given below:

questions.text: Fate of the blastomeres is foretold in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Echinoderms exhibit radial cleavage, but the "fate" of blastomeres is not specifically foretold in this manner.
These organisms share some embryonic features with chordates but don't typically have a fate foretold through blastomeres in the same sense.
Arthropods show a spiral and determination cleavage and there fate of each blastomere is foretold.
Chordates, including vertebrates, exhibit various embryonic development patterns, but the fate of blastomeres in the same predictive manner as in arthropods isn't a defining characteristic.

questions.explanation: Arthropods are proterostomes in which cleavage is determinate (fate of blastomeres is foretold). Each blastomere developed into its special structure.

questions.text: Which one of the following is the primary host of liver fluke?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Humans can be infected by the liver fluke, but they are not the primary host.
Snails serve as an intermediate host in the life cycle of the liver fluke but not the primary host.
Fasciola (Liver luke): It is an endoparasite in sheep and occasionally in human beings.
Dogs can also be infected by the liver fluke, but they are not the primary host in the life cycle of the parasite.

questions.explanation: Sheep is the primary host of liver fluke.

questions.text: Which one of the following is an example of a free-living carnivorous flatworm?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A parasitic flatworm that primarily resides in the liver of various mammals, including humans.
Another parasitic flatworm that lives in the digestive system of vertebrates, including humans, absorbs nutrients.
A few species are free-living and found in freshwater, for example, Dugesia.
A parasitic flatworm causing schistosomiasis, living in blood vessels and reproducing within a human host.

questions.explanation: Dugesia are camivores, and they eat other small invertebrates and dead or decaying animals.

questions.text: Ascaris is which one of the following?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Parasites that live on the external surface of their host organisms. Ascaris is not an ectoparasite.
Ascaris primarily infests the intestines, not the respiratory tract.
Ascaris lumbricoides is an intestinal parasite of man.
Ascaris does not primarily infect the urinogenital tract; its main location is the intestines.

questions.explanation: Adult Ascaris worms inhabit the lumen of the small intestine, usually in the jejunum or ileus. They have a life span of 10 months to 2 years and then are passed in the stool.

questions.text: Polymorphism is a feature exhibited by members of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In coelenterates(cnidaria) , both the polyp and medusa are constructed on the same scheme. The colonial form of life shows alternation of generation and polymorphism.
These are sponges, and while they display diversity, they don't typically exhibit polymorphism in the same way as coelenterates.
This phylum encompasses insects, spiders, and crustaceans, showing significant diversity but not the same type of polymorphism as observed in coelenterates.
Flatworms exhibit diversity in form and lifestyle, but polymorphism, as observed in coelenterates, is not a prominent feature in this phylum.

questions.explanation: Polymorphism refers to the occurrence of structurally and functionally more than two different types of individuals within the same organism. It is a characteristic feature of Cnidarians (Coelenterates), particularly the polyp and medusa forms, or of zooids within colonial organisms like those in hydrozoa.

questions.text: _is a triploblastic organism.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: These are diploblastic organisms, composed of two germ layers: the ectoderm and endoderm.
Tapeworm is included in grade bilateria which are triploblastic.
Sea anemones are diploblastic, formed from two germ layers.
Like sea anemones, corals are also diploblastic organisms with two germ layers.

questions.explanation: The body of a Platyhelminthes (or tapeworm) consists of not only the ectoderm and the endoderm but also the mesoderm.

questions.text: In arthropods, the body cavity is in the form of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A true body cavity lined with mesoderm. Arthropods typically lack a coelom.
A body cavity partially lined with mesoderm. Arthropods generally don't have a pseudocoelom.
The coelom is not present as the main body cavity of Arthropoda. Instead, a haemocoel has developed.
The primitive body cavity or primitive gut, not directly related to the body cavity form in arthropods.

questions.explanation: In arthropods, the body cavity is in the form of haemocoel.

questions.text: _is also called liver fluke.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A free-living flatworm is commonly known as a planarian, not referred to as the liver fluke.

With few exceptions, the Platyhelminthes are parasites, mostly endoparasites, i.e., live inside their hosts. The most common examples are Taenia solium (tapeworm), Fasciola hepatica (liver luke), and Schistosoma (blood luke).
These are tapeworms, not liver flukes. They are segmented parasites found in the intestines of their hosts.
A marine invertebrate, not related to liver flukes.

questions.explanation: Fasciola hepatica, also known as the common liver fluke lives in bile duct.

questions.text: Name of common gut roundworm parasite of humans and pigs.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ascaris lumbricoides is an intestinal parasite of man.
A species of earthworm, not a parasitic roundworm affecting humans or pigs.
Another species of earthworm, not a parasitic worm affecting humans or pigs.
This is the medicinal leech, not a gut roundworm affecting humans or pigs.

questions.explanation: Ascaris worms inhabit the lumen of the small intestine, usually in the jejunum or ileus. They have a life span of 10 months to 2 years and then are passed in the stool.

questions.text: _is a good example of polymorphism.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: These organisms display some regeneration ability but don't prominently exhibit polymorphism.
In Obelia for example there is asexual as well as sexual reproduction. It has a kind of zooid known as blastostyle which gives rise to individual zooids called medusae by the asexual method.
While they display regeneration and can have different forms, they don't showcase polymorphism to the extent seen in Obelia.
Also known as the Venus' flower basket sponge, it demonstrates intricate skeletal structures but doesn't exhibit polymorphism in the same manner as Obelia.

questions.explanation: Polymorphism refers to the occurrence of structurally and functionally more than two different types of individuals within the same organisms. It is a characteristic icature of Cnidarians (Coelenterates), particularly the polyp and medusa forms, or of zooids within colonial organisms like Obelia.

questions.text: In radial symmetry, all body parts are arranged around the central axis. Radial symmetry represents_mode of life.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Radially symmetrical organisms, like sea anemones and certain jellyfish, are typically sessile, anchored in one place, and not capable of extensive movement.
While some radially symmetrical organisms can move, their movement is often limited compared to organisms with bilateral symmetry.
Radial symmetry doesn't specifically relate to streamlined forms, as these organisms generally aren't built for swift movement.
Radially symmetrical organisms can be parasitic, but this mode of life isn't particularly tied to radial symmetry.

questions.explanation: In radial symmetry all body parts are arranged around the central axis. Radial symmetry represents sessile mode of life.

questions.text: Pseudo-coelomates have a body cavity but it is not true coelom. Which one of the following is included in the group?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: They are acoelomates, lacking a true body cavity.
Earthworms are true coelomates, possessing a well-developed coelom.
Tapeworms are also acoelomates, lacking a true body cavity.
Ascaris lumbricoides is an intestinal parasite of man that is included in pseudo-coelomates.

questions.explanation: Ascaris belong to the phylum Nematoda of super phylum Aschelminthes. They have a cylindrical body without showing any metamerism, a pseudococl (false coelom) and a complete digestive tract lined by endodermal epithelium.

questions.text: Taenia is an endoparasite of humans, pigs, and cattle, which belongs to the phylum:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phylum containing jellyfish, corals, and sea anemones, are characterized by stinging cells called cnidocytes. Taenia is not part of this phylum.
This phylum includes segmented worms like earthworms and leeches. Taenia does not belong to this group.
A now outdated group that included roundworms like Ascaris. Taenia is not classified under this phylum.
With few exceptions the Platyhelminthes are parasites, mostly endoparasites, i.e., live inside their hosts. The most common examples are Taenia solium (tapeworm)

questions.explanation: Taenia is a genus of tapeworms (a type of Platyhelminthes) that includes some important parasites of livestock.

questions.text: The body of_ consists of segments called Proglottids, which contain mainly sex organs.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Flatworms with a simple body structure, lack segments like proglottids. They reproduce asexually through regeneration.
A type of parasitic flatworm (a fluke) that primarily infests the liver and doesn't have the segmented body structure seen in proglottids.
Ascaris is a roundworm, not a flatworm, and doesn't possess proglottids or segmented structures.

An endoparasite of humans, cattle and pig, that completes its life cycle in two hosts. The intermediate host is pig or cattle. The body is ribbon-like and divided into segments called proglottids which contain mainly sex organs.

questions.explanation: The major part of the tapeworm is called strobila and it consists of seginents, proglottids. They each contain both male and female reproductive organs.

questions.text: _is a common parasite of the intestine of humans and pigs, which belongs to phylum nematode.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is a tapeworm that typically infects the intestines of humans. It belongs to the phylum Platyhelminthes, not Nematoda.
This roundworm is a common parasite of the intestine in both humans and pigs, and it belongs to the phylum Nematoda.
These parasites, causing schistosomiasis, belong to the phylum Platyhelminthes. They are not nematodes but rather flukes.
This is a liver fluke, a parasite that infects the liver, and it belongs to the phylum Platyhelminthes, not Nematoda.

questions.explanation: Ascaris worms inhabit the lumen of the small intestine of human and pig.

questions.text: Snails are the intermediate hosts in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is an endoparasite in sheep and occasionally in human beings. It has suckers used for attachment to host tissue. It completes its life cycle in two hosts, a snail, sheep, or man. It lives in the bile duct of its hosts.
Snails serve as an intermediate host for different species of Schistosoma. They play a crucial role in the life cycle of these parasitic flatworms.
Pigs are the intermediate hosts for Taenia solium, not snails.
The larvae of Ancylostoma duodenale, a hookworm, typically develop in the soil and can infect humans through skin contact. They don't utilize snails as intermediate hosts.

questions.explanation: Snails are the intermediate hosts of Fasciola hepatica.

questions.text: _is an intestinal parasite of man belonging to phylum Nematoda.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is a tapeworm that commonly infests the intestine, but it belongs to the phylum Platyhelminthes, not Nematoda.
Ascaris lumbricoides is an intestinal parasite of man included in nematoda.
There is no recognized parasite or term by this name.
This parasite is not a nematode; it's a blood fluke, belonging to the phylum Platyhelminthes.

questions.explanation: Ascaris lumbricoides worms inhabit the lumen of the small intestine of human and pig.

questions.text: The following group is the an example of acoelomates:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This group includes flatworms, many of which are acoelomates. They lack a true body cavity between the gut and body wall.
This group once included roundworms like Ascaris but has been deprecated and is no longer recognized as a valid phylum.
Molluscs have a true coelom, a fluid-filled body cavity between the gut and body wall, so they are not acoelomates.
Annelids, such as earthworms and leeches, have a true coelom, which differentiates them from acoelomates.

questions.explanation: Examples of acoelomates are found phylum Platyhelminthes, commonly known as flatworms. These invertebrate animals are unsegmented worms with bilateral symmetry.

questions.text: The larva formed during the life cycle of Annelida is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This larval stage is seen in some freshwater bivalves like mussels and freshwater clams.
Bipinnaria larvae are part of the life cycle of echinoderms, such as starfish and sea urchins.

The common mode of reproduction is sexual. Most annelids (Earthworms, leeches) are hermaphrodites. In some annelids (e.g., Nereis) the sexes are separate, the fertilization is external and a free swimming trochophore larva is produced during the life cycle.
Tornaria larvae are a type of free-swimming larva seen in some species of hemichordates, like acorn worms.

questions.explanation: Trochophore, also called trochosphere, small, translucent, free-swimming larva characteristic of marine annelids and most groups of mollusks.

questions.text: Platyhelminthes are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Organisms with two germ layers are diploblastic, and bilateral symmetry refers to a body plan where the right and left sides mirror each other. However, Platyhelminthes have three germ layers, so this option doesn't accurately describe them.

The Platyhelminthes are triploblastic acoelomates. There is development of a third layer, then mesoderm, which separates the ectoderm and endoderm. The Platyhelminthes exhibit bilateral symmetry, and body is unsegmented.
Radial symmetry involves body parts arranged around a central point, which doesn't apply to Platyhelminthes, and they are triploblastic, not radially symmetric.
Platyhelminthes are bilaterally symmetric and triploblastic, not radially symmetric or diploblastic.

questions.explanation: Platyhelminthes have the following important characteristics: They are triploblastic, acoelomate, and bilaterally symmetrical. They may be free-living or parasites.The body has a soft covering with or without cilia.Their body is dorsoventrally flattened without any segments and appears like a leaf.

questions.text: Which group of organisms has the following features?1. Three pairs of jointed legs2. Three-part segmented body3. One pair of antennae
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Arachnids have four pairs of legs, not three, and their body is divided into two segments - the cephalothorax and abdomen.
Crustaceans typically have a varying number of legs, often more than three pairs and their bodies can have multiple segments.

In insects, there is a pair of antennae and compound eyes on the head. insects have three distinct regions head, thorax, and abdomen. The thorax has three segments in which are present three pairs of jointed legs and in many one or two pairs of wings.
Myriapods, like centipedes and millipedes, have numerous pairs of legs and a segmented body, but they do not possess just three pairs of legs or a three-part body plan.

questions.explanation: The explanation is given below:

questions.text: Platyhelminthes means:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Platyhelminthes is a phylum of invertebrates that includes flatworms. "Platy" means flat, and "helminths" refers to worms. Therefore, "Platyhelminthes" directly translates to "flatworms."
Roundworms belong to the phylum Nematoda, which is a distinct group from Platyhelminthes. Roundworms have a cylindrical, round body, unlike the flat body of flatworms.
Segmented worms belong to the phylum Annelida, characterized by their body divisions into segments, unlike the largely unsegmented flatworms.
The term "Platyhelminthes" does have a specific meaning, referring to a group of flatworms, so this isn't the correct option.

questions.explanation: Word Platyhelminthes (derived from the Greek platy, meaning "flat" while helminth meaning "worm") are a phylum of relatively simple bilaterian, unsegmented, soft-bodied invertebrates.

questions.text: Reptiles flourished throughout_era.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The Cenozoic era began around 66 million years ago and continues to the present.
The Devonian period occurred in the Paleozoic era, around 419 to 359 million years ago.
The Proterozoic eon was an ancient geological time extending from around 2.5 billion to 541 million years ago.
Reptiles flourished throughout Mesozoic era (225-65 million years).

questions.explanation: The Mesozoic Era is an interval of geological time from about 252 to 66 million years ago. It is also called the Age of Reptiles.

questions.text: Birds do not have teeth so the function of teeth is performed by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Birds use their beaks for various functions, including grasping, eating, and sometimes breaking down food, but they don't have teeth.
The pharynx is part of the digestive and respiratory systems but doesn't directly perform the function of teeth.
The stomach is where digestion primarily takes place, breaking down food using acid and enzymes, but it doesn't substitute for the lack of teeth in birds.
Since birds do not have teeth they have developed a thick muscular structure (Gizzard) which is used for crushing food.

questions.explanation: Birds do not have teeth Birds "chew" their food in their gizzard.

questions.text: Which of them is not poikilotherm?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Spiny anteaters(Echidnas) are mammals and, like most mammals, are endothermic, meaning they can regulate their body temperature internally. They are not poikilothermic (cold-blooded).
Lizards are ectothermic, or poikilothermic, meaning their body temperature fluctuates with the surrounding environment.
Crocodiles are also ectothermic, or poikilothermic, relying on external sources to regulate their body temperature.
Snakes are ectothermic animals, depending on external sources to control their body temperature. They are poikilothermic.

questions.explanation: A poikilotherm is an animal whose internal temperature varies considerably. It is the opposite of a homeotherm, an animal which maintains thennal homeostasis. Spiny ant eaters belong to homeotherm.

questions.text: The lower jaw is composed of only one large bone:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Reptiles have lower jaws composed of multiple bones, not just one large bone.
Amphibians also have lower jaws made up of several bones, not a single large bone.
Fish belonging to this class, such as sharks and rays, have lower jaws that are indeed made of cartilage, but they are not a single large bone.
The lower jaw in mammals is composed of only one large bone and articulates directly with skull.

questions.explanation: The lower Jaw of mammals consists of only one bone, the dentary, and the jaw hinge connects the dentary to the squamosal (flat) part of the temporal bone in the skull. Working together, these muscles permit up-and-down and side-to-side movements of the jaw,making chewing possible which is unique to mammals.

questions.text: Opossum and koala bear belongs to the sub-class:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This subclass includes the egg-laying mammals, such as the platypus and echidna.
Eutherian mammals are placental mammals, which give birth to relatively developed young ones.
This subclass comprises marsupial mammals, like opossums and koalas, which give birth to relatively undeveloped young that further develop in a pouch.
Monotremes are egg-laying mammals, such as the platypus and echidna.

questions.explanation: Metatheria includes marsupials that possess a pouch and give birth to partially-developed young ones. Well-known marsupials include kangaroos, wallabies, koalas, opossums, woinbats, Tasmanian devils, and the extinct Thylacme.

questions.text: The table shows some characteristics of four different vertebrates. Which vertebrate is a reptile?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chordate's skin has embedded dermal scales which may be ganoid, cycloid, or ctenoid scales. Fins both, median (single) or paired and have rays of cartilage or bone.
Reptiles have dry scaly skin which is adapted to land life. Most reptiles have better-developed limbs well adapted for efficient locomotion.
Amphibians, like frogs, toads, and salamanders, possess legs for locomotion on land or in water but typically do not have scales or hair.
The vertebrates possessing legs and hair but lacking fins and scales are mammals.

questions.explanation: Characteristics of Reptiles:✓ Reptiles are four-legged vertebrate animals.✓ Most reptiles lay eggs.The skin of reptiles is covered with scales. ✓ Reptiles have cold-blooded metabolisms. ✓ Reptiles breathe with the aid of lungs.

questions.text: All chordates share these features except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The notochord is a flexible rod-like structure that provides support to the body and is present in the embryonic stage of all chordates. It is a defining characteristic of chordates.

Gill slits are openings in the pharynx (throat) region that allow water to pass through, and they are associated with the respiratory and filter-feeding systems. Gill slits are a common feature in the pharyngeal region of chordates, particularly in the early stages of development.

A post-anal tail is an extension of the body beyond the anal opening. This feature is present in the embryonic stage of many chordates and is a characteristic shared by all chordates, at least during some point in their development.

It includes animals that have a cranium in which the brain is enclosed. It includes animals with vertebral columns which means that all chordates in this, group are under subphylum Vertebrata and are therefore vertebrates

questions.explanation: Vertebral column is present only in vertebrates.

questions.text: The central nervous system of chordates is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The term "ventral" refers to the underside or belly side of an organism. In chordates, the central nervous system is not located ventrally. Instead, it is typically positioned dorsally, along the back.

This option suggests that the central nervous system is both ventral and hollow. While the neural tube, which gives rise to the CNS, begins as a hollow structure in the early embryonic stages, the mature CNS is not ventrally positioned. It develops dorsally.
The lateral sides of an organism are the sides. The central nervous system is not typically located laterally; instead, it is positioned along the dorsal (back) side.

This option is correct. The central nervous system of chordates is dorsal (along the back) and initially develops from a hollow structure called the neural tube. The neural tube gives rise to the brain and spinal cord.

questions.explanation: The central nervous system of all chordates is dorsal and hollow.

questions.text: Bulanoglossus and Saccoglossus are members of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Echinoderms include organisms like sea stars, sea urchins, and sea cucumbers. They are characterized by a unique water vascular system and radial symmetry. Bulanoglossus and Saccoglossus are not members of Echinodermata.
Annelids are segmented worms, including earthworms and leeches. Bulanoglossus and Saccoglossus are not members of Annelida.
Nematodes are roundworms with cylindrical bodies. Bulanoglossus and Saccoglossus are not members of Nematoda.
Because of their close relationship to chordates these animals are called prechordates. The common examples of this phylum are Balanoglossus and Saccoglossus.

questions.explanation: Balanoglossus and Saccoglossus belong to phylum hemichordata.

questions.text: Direct ancestors of grade radiata are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Parazoa is a subkingdom that includes simple, asymmetrical animals, such as sponges. Sponges are considered the most primitive animals and are part of the phylum Porifera.
Porifera refers to sponges. While sponges are primitive animals, they are not considered the direct ancestors of grade Radiata. Instead, they represent a basal group within the animal kingdom.
Protozoa are single-celled, microscopic organisms. They are not direct ancestors of grade Radiata. Protozoa belong to the kingdom Protista, and animals with true tissues (Eumetazoa) evolved later.

Eumetazoa includes animals with true tissues. The direct ancestors of grade Radiata are generally considered to be within the group Eumetazoa, as this group includes animals with radial symmetry and tissues organized into germ layers. Radiata is a grade within Eumetazoa that includes animals like cnidarians (jellyfish, corals, sea anemones) and ctenophores (comb jellies).

questions.explanation: "The explanation for this question will be added soon!".

questions.text: All are characteristics of mollusks except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The body is unsegmented and soft.
Molluscs are triploblastic coelomates which exhibit bilateral symmetry.
Molluscs are triploblastic coelomates that exhibit bilateral symmetry.
Mollusks are triploblastic coelomates that exhibit bilateral symmetry. The body is unsegmented and soft. They are not pseudocoelomates.

questions.explanation: "The explanation for this question will be added soon!".

questions.text: The body cavity is truly divided in all of the following except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The body can be divided into head, a ventral muscular footand a dorsal visceral mass containing most of the internal organs.

Arthropods are variable structurally. Some are worm-like centipedes while the others are lying insects with the body divided into distinct regions, the head, thorax and abdomen. The body is covered with waterproof chitinous cuticle secreted by the epidermis.
The body becomes divided transversely into a number ofsimilar parts or segments. The subdivisions may be indicated externally by constrictions of the body surface.

The animals included in this group have elongated worm-like bodies with pointed ends. The nematodes are triploblastic and pseudocoelomates. One end of the body is anterior, however, the head is not marked and there are no special sense organs at this end.

questions.explanation: "The explanation for this question will be added soon!".

questions.text: Highly organized mesoderm represents:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mesoderm gives rise to the muscular, skeletal, blood-vascular, and reproductive systems.

Radial symmetry refers to symmetry around a central axis. While mesoderm organization is crucial for the development of certain structures in radially symmetrical animals (like cnidarians), the term "highly organized mesoderm" is more commonly associated with animals displaying bilateral symmetry.
The mesoderm is involved in the formation of both simple and complex organs, but the term "highly organized mesoderm" suggests a higher degree of complexity in organ formation.

Highly organized mesoderm is indeed associated with animals exhibiting bilateral symmetry. Bilateral symmetry means that an organism can be divided into two equal halves along a single plane. Animals with bilateral symmetry often have more complex and specialized organ systems.

questions.explanation: "The explanation for this question will be added soon!".

questions.text: Which of the following is the outer layer of the mesoderm?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The mesoderm splits into outer parietal layer which under lines the body wall and the visceral layer which covers the alimentary canal and the cavity between them is the true coelom.
The visceral layer is the inner layer of the mesoderm. It is associated with the development of organs and structures that are more internal, such as the lining of certain body cavities.
"Intestinal" is not a term commonly used to describe layers of the mesoderm. In the context of embryonic development, the parietal and visceral layers are more relevant.

While muscles originate from the mesoderm, the term "muscular" is not used to describe specific layers of the mesoderm. The differentiation into specific types of muscles occurs within the mesoderm but is not referred to as a separate layer.

questions.explanation: "The explanation for this question will be added soon!".

questions.text: In animals, a reproductive system developed from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The ectoderm is one of the three germ layers in the early embryo. It gives rise to structures such as the skin, nervous system, and epidermal tissues. While the nervous system plays a role in reproductive processes, the reproductive system itself does not develop directly from the ectoderm.

The blastoderm is not a specific germ layer but rather a stage in early embryonic development. During blastoderm formation, the embryo is divided into layers, including the three germ layers (ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm). The reproductive system develops from one of these germ layers.
Mesoderm gives rise to muscular, skeletal, and reproductivesystems.

The endoderm is another of the three germ layers, and it contributes to the development of internal organs such as the digestive tract. While the endoderm indirectly influences the reproductive system through its role in supporting overall organismal development, the reproductive organs themselves primarily originate from the mesoderm.

questions.explanation: "The explanation for this question will be added soon!".

questions.text: Which one of the following is correct about parazoa?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Parazoans, or Parazoa, typically exhibit asymmetry or simple, irregular body shapes rather than radial symmetry. Radial symmetry is more characteristic of animals like cnidarians (e.g., jellyfish).

Parazoans are generally considered to be diploblastic, meaning they have two germ layers (ectoderm and endoderm) during embryonic development. Triploblastic animals have three germ layers, and this characteristic is not applicable to parazoans.
The simplest of the animals belong to subkingdom Parazoa (phylum Porifera). These animals lack tissues organised into organs and have indeterminate shape, and are asymmetrical.

Indeterminate cleavage is a characteristic of certain embryonic development patterns, typically found in deuterostomes. Parazoans do not undergo cleavage in the same way as more complex animals with determinate cleavage.

questions.text: During embryonic development the mouth is formed at some distance anterior to the blastopore and the blastopore forms the anus in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: When blastopore develops into anus then these animals are called deuterostomates. Echinoderms and chordates are deuterostomates.
Molluscs are protostomates , in them blastopore develops into mouth .
Nematods are pseudocoelomates therefore they are not grouped into protostomes and deuterostomes.
These are also pseudocoelomates.

questions.explanation: A. When blastopore develops into anus then these animals are called deuterostomates. Echinoderms and chordates are deuterostomates. B. Molluscs are protostomates , in them blastopore develops into mouth . C. Nematods are pseudocoelomates therefore they are not grouped into protostomes and deuterostomes. D. These are also pseudocoelomates.

questions.text: Saclike digestive system is present in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cnidarians are diploblastic so they lack proper digestive system.
Platyhelminthes has flat worms. They are triploblastic and have mouth which opens into a gut but no anus so their digestive system is termed as sac like.
Arthropods have tube like digestive system with proper mouth and anus.
Chordates have tube like digestive system with proper mouth and anus at opposite ends.

questions.explanation: Platyhelminthes has flat worms. They are triploblastic and have mouth which opens into a gut but no anus so their digestive system is termed as sac like.

questions.text: Which one of the following is an example of a radial organism?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Planaria is flat worm (platyhelminthes) which are bilaterally symmetrical.
Humans are also bilaterally symmetrical.

Sea anemone belongs to phylum Cnidaria, which are radially symmetrical.Radial symmetry is a property of an item (e.g., a shape or an animal) that can be divided into two matching halves by many different lines, which all intersect one another at a single point in the center.
Insects belong to phylum arthropoda which are bilaterally symmetrical.

questions.explanation: Sea anemone belongs to phylum Cnidaria, which are radially symmetrical.Radial symmetry is a property of an item (e.g., a shape or an animal) that can be divided into two matching halves by many different lines, which all intersect one another at a single point in the center.

questions.text: Sharks and rays are included in class:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cyclostomes are jawless fishes. They are grouped in sub phylum Agnatha.
Osteichthyes are bony fishes.
Chondrichthyes are cartilagenous fishes having exoskeleton of cartilage. Skates and rays are cartilagenous fishes.
Tetrapoda is the group of vertebrates having two pairs of pentadactyl limbs.

questions.explanation: Chondrichthyes are cartilagenous fishes having exoskeleton of cartilage. Skates and rays are cartilagenous fishes.

questions.text: Which of the following does not have specialized respiratory organs?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hydra belong to the phylum Cnidarians.They have no respiratory, excretory and circulatory system.
Cockroaches belong to the phylum insecta having proper respiratory system.
Birds have proper respiratory organs.
Hydras donot have but cockroaches do have respiratory system.

questions.explanation: Hydra has neither blood and blood vessels, nor organs of excretion and respiration, which are carried on individually by each cell. Due to the thinness of the body wall and circulation of water in the gastrovascular cavity, most of the cells of the body remain freely exposed to the surrounding water.

questions.text: The coelom is a cavity lined by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Coelom is lined by mesodermal cells.
This is factually incorrect. Epidermis is the outermost layer.
Inner layer of cells called endoderm.
Outer layer of cells called ectoderm.

questions.explanation: The coelom is a fluid-filled cavity which is lined by the mesoderm. It is present in between the gastrointestinal tract and the body wall. An organism that has a fluid filled cavity lined by the mesoderm are called coelomates.Roundworms have a cavity which is not lined by the mesoderm, thus they are known as pseudocoelomates. Those organisms that lack a fluid filled cavity are called acoelomates.

questions.text: It is an endoparasite of humans, cattle, and pigs that completes its life cycle in two hosts:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This belongs to phylum platyhelminthes , class Cestoda, being exclusively endoparasite .
This belongs to class Trematoda being endo or ectoparasites .
Aurelia (jelly fish) is not parasite.
Planaria (dugesia) is not parasite.

questions.explanation: This belongs to phylum platyhelminthes, class Cestoda, being exclusively endoparasite. The primary host of tapeworm is human and the secondary host is pig. The most common way to pick up a tapeworm is by eating undercooked meat. If pigs eat the infected feces containing eggs, the eggs hatch and the larvae form into cysts that make their way into the animals' muscles.

questions.text: Tse-Tse fly causes sleeping sickness and skin diseases by transmitting:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Plasmodium causes malaria.
Anopheles causes malaria.
Trypanosoma is transmitted by tse tse fly causing sleeping sickness.
Insects spread many known diseases such as cholera, dysentary.

questions.explanation: African trypanosomiasis, also called sleeping sickness, is a disease caused by a parasite. People can get this parasite when an infected Tsetse fly bites them. Symptoms include fatigue, high fever, headaches, and muscle aches. If the disease is not treated, it can cause death.

questions.text: Book lungs are present in arthropods for the exchange of gases in class:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: They donot have book lungs.
They donot have book lungs.
They donot have book lungs.
Book lungs are present in scorpiions, spiders . They belong to class Arachnida.(fact)

questions.explanation: Book lungs are a rudimentary type of lung typically found in arachnids, such as scorpions and spiders, and in horseshoe crabs.

questions.text: A parasite living inside the body of the host is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Parasite living outside of its host called ectoparasite.
Parasite which can complete its life cycle with or without host.
Parasite which completes its life cycle with host otherwise without host it cannot complete its life cycle.
Parasite living inside of its host.

questions.explanation: Endoparasites are parasites that live in the tissues and organs of their hosts, such as tapeworms, flukes, and protozoans of vertebrates.

questions.text: Which of the following is the exclusive character of mammals?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This term refers to the ability of an organism to maintain a relatively constant body temperature, usually warmer than the surrounding environment. Mammals are generally homeothermic, meaning they can regulate their body temperature internally.

This term is the opposite of homeothermic. Poikilothermic animals, like reptiles and amphibians, have body temperatures that fluctuate with their environment, and they cannot internally regulate their temperature.
Hair is a defining characteristic of mammals. Most mammals have hair or fur covering some or all of their bodies. This feature distinguishes them from other animal groups.

Mammals have a four-chambered heart, just like birds. This type of heart allows for efficient separation of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood, which is crucial for supporting their high metabolic rates.

questions.explanation: Hair is a defining characteristic of mammals. Most mammals have hair or fur covering some or all of their bodies. This feature distinguishes them from other animal groups.

questions.text: Fasciola is endoparasite of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fasciola does not primarily parasitize the colon. It is not typically found in the large intestine
Fasciola is not a parasite of the small intestine. It does not inhabit the digestive tract.
This is the correct answer. Fasciola hepatica, the common liver fluke, is an endoparasite that infects the liver. It burrows into the liver tissues, causing damage and various symptoms.
Fasciola often moves from the liver into the bile ducts, where it can cause blockages and further health problems. So, while it primarily infects the liver, it can also affect the bile ducts.

questions.explanation: Fasciola (Liver luke) : It is an endoparasite in sheep and occasionally in human beings. It has suckers used for attachment to host tissue. It completes its life cycle in two hosts, a snail, sheep or man. It lives in the bile duct of its hosts.

questions.text: Trypanosoma is transmitted in human beings by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Plasmodium is the parasite responsible for causing malaria, and it is transmitted to humans through the bite of infected female Anopheles mosquitoes. It is not associated with Trypanosoma transmission.
House flies are not known to transmit Trypanosoma to humans. They are involved in the transmission of various other diseases, but not Trypanosoma.
Anopheles mosquitoes transmit Plasmodium, not Trypanosoma. They are responsible for the spread of malaria.

This is the correct answer. The tsetse fly is a vector for Trypanosoma parasites that cause African trypanosomiasis, also known as sleeping sickness. When an infected tsetse fly bites a human, it can transmit the Trypanosoma parasites, leading to the disease.

questions.explanation: Trypanosoma, the cause of sleeping sickness and skin diseases. The common house ly carries disease causing organisms to contaminate food and cause cholera, hepatitis etc. Some species of Trypanosoma cause diseases in cattle, also.

questions.text: During development, in an animal, the mesoderm layer gives rise to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The nervous system primarily develops from the ectoderm layer, not the mesoderm. The ectoderm gives rise to the neural tube, which eventually forms the brain and spinal cord

This is the correct answer. The mesoderm layer contributes to the development of the skeleton (bone and cartilage) and the muscular system. Mesodermal cells differentiate into various types of connective tissues, which include those forming the skeleton and muscles.
The lining of the alimentary canal, which includes the digestive tract, is derived from the endoderm layer, not the mesoderm.
The mouth is primarily formed from the ectoderm and endoderm layers, with contributions from the mesoderm for the muscles around the mouth but not for the formation of the mouth itself.

questions.explanation: The mesoderm layer contributes to the development of the skeleton (bone and cartilage) and the muscular system. Mesodermal cells differentiate into various types of connective tissues, which include those forming the skeleton and muscles.

questions.text: Fasciola is the name given to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tape worm refers to a group of parasitic flatworms that typically live in the intestines of vertebrates. They are known for their segmented body structure.

This is the correct answer. Fasciola, specifically Fasciola hepatica, is a type of liver fluke. It is a parasitic flatworm that infects the liver and sometimes the bile ducts of mammals, including humans.
Planaria is not related to Fasciola. Planaria is a non-parasitic flatworm often used in biology labs for regeneration studies. It is not a liver fluke.
Earthworms are completely different organisms and belong to the group of annelid worms. They are not related to liver flukes or Fasciola.

questions.explanation: Fasciola, specifically Fasciola hepatica, is a type of liver fluke. It is a parasitic flatworm that infects the liver and sometimes the bile ducts of mammals, including humans.

questions.text: Polymorphism is a characteristic feature of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Polymorphism is not a prominent feature of sponges. These are simple aquatic animals with limited differentiation into different forms.
Annelids, such as earthworms and leeches, do exhibit polymorphism to some extent, especially in terms of body segments and specialized structures for different functions.
Polymorphism is a notable feature of cnidarians. Many cnidarians have multiple forms within their life cycle, including the polyp and medusa stages in some species.
Nematodes generally do not exhibit extensive polymorphism compared to some other groups. They have a relatively simple body structure.

questions.explanation: Polymorphism is a notable feature of cnidarians. Many cnidarians have multiple forms within their life cycle, including the polyp and medusa stages in some species.

questions.text: The cavity between the body wall and the alimentary canal is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The coelom is a body cavity found in many animals, including vertebrates. It is located between the body wall and the alimentary canal (digestive tract) and is lined with mesoderm.

The endoderm is one of the primary germ layers in embryonic development. It gives rise to the innermost tissues and organs, including the lining of the alimentary canal. However, it is not the cavity itself.
The mesoderm is another primary germ layer, and it gives rise to various structures, including the coelom, which is the body cavity between the body wall and the alimentary canal in many animals.
Mesoglea is a gelatinous, non-cellular layer found in some simple aquatic animals like jellyfish. It is not associated with the body cavity or the alimentary canal.

questions.explanation: The coelom is a body cavity found in many animals, including vertebrates. It is located between the body wall and the alimentary canal (digestive tract) and is lined with mesoderm.

questions.text: The excretory system in Platyhelminthes is in the form of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Flame cells, also known as protonephridia, are the excretory structures found in Platyhelminthes. These cells have a tubule with a ciliated structure that resembles a flame. They help regulate osmotic balance and remove waste products.
Metanephridia are excretory structures found in some other invertebrates, like annelids. They are not typically associated with Platyhelminthes.
Malpighian tubules are found in insects and some other arthropods. They play a role in excretion and osmoregulation but are not present in Platyhelminthes.
Kidneys are complex excretory organs found in vertebrates, including humans. They are not present in Platyhelminthes.

questions.explanation: Flame cells, also known as protonephridia, are the excretory structures found in Platyhelminthes. These cells have a tubule with a ciliated structure that resembles a flame. They help regulate osmotic balance and remove waste products.

questions.text: Amphibians evolved from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cartilaginous fishes, like sharks and rays, are a distinct group of fishes characterized by their skeleton made of cartilage. Amphibians did not directly evolve from cartilaginous fishes.

Amphibians are believed to have evolved from early tetrapod amphibians, which were the first vertebrates to colonize land. These early amphibians eventually gave rise to reptiles, making reptiles a more distant evolutionary relative of amphibians.
Dipnoi, also known as lungfishes, are a group of fish that can breathe air and are adapted to survive in oxygen-poor water. They are not direct ancestors of amphibians.
Cyclostomata refers to jawless fish, including lampreys and hagfishes. While they are some of the earliest vertebrates, they are not the direct ancestors of amphibians.

questions.explanation: Dipnoi, also known as lungfishes, are a group of fish that can breathe air and are adapted to survive in oxygen-poor water. They are not direct ancestors of amphibians.

questions.text: Amnion is not present around the embryo of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Reptiles do have an amnion around the embryo.

In amphibians, the embryo is not surrounded by an amnion. The amnion is a membrane that forms a fluid-filled sac around the embryo in reptiles, birds, and mammals. This fluid-filled sac provides protection and helps regulate the embryo's temperature. However, in amphibians, this structure is absent, making option B the correct choice.
Birds do have an amnion around the embryo.
Mammals do have an amnion around the embryo.

questions.explanation: In amphibians, the embryo is not surrounded by an amnion. The amnion is a membrane that forms a fluid-filled sac around the embryo in reptiles, birds, and mammals. This fluid-filled sac provides protection and helps regulate the embryo's temperature. However, in amphibians, this structure is absent, making option B the correct choice.

questions.text: In_both ovaries and oviducts are functional.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In Robins, only the left ovary and oviduct are functional.
In Kestrels, only the left ovary and oviduct are functional.
In Eagles, only the left ovary and oviduct are functional.
In Kingfishers, both ovaries and oviducts are functional.

questions.explanation: In Kingfishers, both ovaries and oviducts are functional.

questions.text: Archaeopteryx is a connecting link between:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because Archaeopteryx does not connect reptiles and mammals. Mammals have distinct characteristics that differentiate them from both reptiles and birds, and Archaeopteryx shares more features with birds.

Archaeopteryx is considered a connecting link between reptiles and birds due to its combination of reptilian and avian features. It had feathered wings like birds but also retained reptilian characteristics like teeth and a bony tail. This transitional fossil provides evidence for the evolutionary transition from dinosaurs to modern birds.

This option is incorrect because Archaeopteryx does not bridge the gap between birds and mammals. Birds and mammals are two separate evolutionary branches with different sets of characteristics, and Archaeopteryx is specifically associated with the reptile-to-bird transition.

This option is incorrect because Archaeopteryx is not related to the transition between amphibians and fishes. Amphibians and fishes are part of a different evolutionary lineage, and Archaeopteryx is unrelated to their evolutionary history.

questions.explanation: Archaeopteryx is considered a connecting link between reptiles and birds due to its combination of reptilian and avian features. It had feathered wings like birds but also retained reptilian characteristics like teeth and a bony tail. This transitional fossil provides evidence for the evolutionary transition from dinosaurs to modern birds.

questions.text: In some coelenterates, there are special feeding zooids which are called_which perform only the function of nutrition for the whole colony:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: These are reproductive zooids responsible for producing gametes (sex cells) in some colonial organisms. They are not involved in nutrition.

Gastrozooids are specialized feeding zooids in some coelenterates (such as certain colonial cnidarians like hydroids). They perform the function of nutrition for the entire colony by capturing and digesting prey. These zooids are responsible for obtaining and processing food.
This term is not commonly used in the context of coelenterates or their feeding mechanisms. It does not accurately describe any specific specialized zooid.

Medusazoids could refer to the medusa stage of cnidarians, which is a free-swimming jellyfish-like form. While medusae can capture prey, they are not specialized feeding zooids within a colony; rather, they are a distinct life stage in the cnidarian life cycle.

questions.explanation: Gastrozooids are specialized feeding zooids in some coelenterates (such as certain colonial cnidarians like hydroids). They perform the function of nutrition for the entire colony by capturing and digesting prey. These zooids are responsible for obtaining and processing food.

questions.text: Ascaris is the endoparasite of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ascaris is an endoparasitic roundworm that primarily resides in the small intestine of its host. It is known as an intestinal parasite.
Ascaris primarily inhabits the small intestine, not the large intestine.
Ascaris does not typically reside in the rectum. Its primary location is the small intestine.
Ascaris is not found in the appendix. It prefers the small intestine as its primary habitat.

questions.explanation: Ascaris is an endoparasitic roundworm that primarily resides in the small intestine of its host. It is known as an intestinal parasite.

questions.text: One of the reasons for the success of phylum Arthropoda is its:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Arthropods exhibit an incredible diversity of organisms, including insects, arachnids, crustaceans, and more. This diversity has contributed significantly to their evolutionary success, allowing them to adapt to various environments and ecological niches.
This is a feature found in insects, not a reason for the success of Arthropoda as a phylum. Malpighian tubules are excretory structures that help insects conserve water and eliminate waste.
This option refers to a different phylum entirely, which includes cartilaginous fish like sharks and rays. It is not relevant to the success of Arthropoda.
Mammals belong to a different phylum (Chordata) and are not directly related to the success of Arthropoda.

questions.explanation: Arthropods exhibit an incredible diversity of organisms, including insects, arachnids, crustaceans, and more. This diversity has contributed significantly to their evolutionary success, allowing them to adapt to various environments and ecological niches.

questions.text: Pigment cells called chromatophores are present in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While reptiles do have pigment cells called chromatophores, they are more commonly associated with amphibians.
Chromatophores are pigment cells present in amphibians like frogs, salamanders, and newts. These cells enable them to change their skin color for camouflage, communication, and thermal regulation.
Chondrichthyes, like sharks and rays, do not typically have chromatophores. They use other mechanisms for coloration.
Mammals do not possess chromatophores. Their skin coloration is primarily determined by melanocytes.

questions.explanation: Chromatophores are pigment cells present in amphibians like frogs, salamanders, and newts. These cells enable them to change their skin color for camouflage, communication, and thermal regulation.

questions.text: The mammal-like reptile that was found as a fossil in Texas:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Archaeopteryx is not a mammal-like reptile; it's a transitional fossil between dinosaurs and birds, found in Germany.
The duckbill platypus is a modern monotreme mammal, not a mammal-like reptile. It's native to Australia.
Varanope is a mammal-like reptile fossil found in Texas. It belongs to a group known as varanopids, which were early synapsids, a lineage that eventually gave rise to mammals.
Opossums are modern marsupial mammals, not ancient reptile fossils. They are found in the America.

questions.explanation: Varanope is a mammal-like reptile fossil found in Texas. It belongs to a group known as varanopids, which were early synapsids, a lineage that eventually gave rise to mammals.

questions.text: The most important function of suckers is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Suckers are not involved in the absorption of blood; this function is typically performed by specialized mouthparts or organs.

Suckers in various organisms, such as in insects or parasites like leeches, are specialized structures primarily designed for attachment. They allow these organisms to adhere to surfaces, including host organisms, for purposes like feeding or reproduction.
Suckers are not primarily designed for ingesting food; organisms usually have separate mouthparts or structures for this purpose.
Suckers are not involved in waste excretion; waste excretion is managed by other specialized organs like kidneys or excretory systems.

questions.explanation: Suckers in various organisms, such as in insects or parasites like leeches, are specialized structures primarily designed for attachment. They allow these organisms to adhere to surfaces, including host organisms, for purposes like feeding or reproduction.

questions.text: The animals of the phylum_are known as schizocoelous.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Animals of the phylum Arthropoda exhibit schizocoelous development, which involves the splitting of the mesoderm to form a coelom. This type of coelom formation is characteristic of arthropods.
Echinoderms develop through enterocoelous development, where the coelom forms from pouches that pinch off the primitive gut.
Poriferans lack a true coelom; they have a cellular level of organization and lack well-defined body cavities.
Chordates typically undergo enterocoelous or schizocoelous development, depending on the subphylum. However, schizocoelous development is not a defining feature of chordates.

questions.explanation: Animals of the phylum Arthropoda exhibit schizocoelous development, which involves the splitting of the mesoderm to form a coelom. This type of coelom formation is characteristic of arthropods.

questions.text: All of the following have no swim bladder except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cyclostomes, do not have a swim bladder. The swim bladder is a gas-filled sac that helps fish to control their buoyancy and maintain their position in the water column.
Chondrichthyes, which include sharks, rays, and chimaeras, do not have a swim bladder. Instead, they rely on their large oily liver to control their buoyancy.
Osteichthyes, which include bony fish, do have a swim bladder. The swim bladder is a gas-filled sac that helps fish to control their buoyancy and maintain their position in the water column.

Amphibians, which include frogs, toads, salamanders, and newts, do not have a swim bladder. Instead, they rely on their muscular limbs and body to control their buoyancy and movement in the water. Some species of amphibians, such as frogs, have webbed feet that help them to swim more efficiently, while others, such as salamanders, have long, slender bodies that allow them to move through the water with ease.

questions.explanation: Osteichthyes, which include bony fish, do have a swim bladder. The swim bladder is a gas-filled sac that helps fish to control their buoyancy and maintain their position in the water column.

questions.text: _is a common feature of all mammals.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Not all mammals are viviparous, which means giving birth to live young. There are two other types of reproduction in mammals: monotremes and marsupials. Monotremes, such as the platypus and echidnas, lay eggs instead of giving birth to live young. Marsupials, such as kangaroos, wallabies, and opossums, give birth to relatively undeveloped young, which then continue to develop outside the womb in a pouch called the marsupium.

Not all mammals have the characteristics of a placenta. A placenta is a specialized organ that connects the developing fetus to the mother's uterus, allowing for the exchange of nutrients, gases, and waste products. However, there are two other types of reproduction in mammals: monotremes and marsupials. Monotremes, such as the platypus and echidnas, lay eggs instead of giving birth to live young, so they do not have a placenta. Marsupials, such as kangaroos, wallabies, and opossums, have a rudimentary placenta that does not fully support fetal development, so the young are born relatively undeveloped and continue to develop outside the womb in a pouch called the marsupium.

Not all mammals have a right aortic arch. The aortic arch is a curved blood vessel that carries oxygen-rich blood from the heart to the rest of the body. In most mammals, including humans, the aortic arch curves to the left as it leaves the heart. However, in some mammals, such as birds and reptiles, the aortic arch curves to the right. In mammals, a right aortic arch is relatively rare and is found primarily in some species of rodents, such as guinea pigs and chinchillas.

Mammary glands are a characteristic of all mammals. These specialized glands produce milk, which is used to nourish and feed the young. The milk is rich in nutrients and antibodies, which help to protect the young from disease and infection. Mammals are named after these glands, which are unique to the class Mammalia.

questions.explanation: Mammary glands are a characteristic of all mammals. These specialized glands produce milk, which is used to nourish and feed the young. The milk is rich in nutrients and antibodies, which help to protect the young from disease and infection. Mammals are named after these glands, which are unique to the class Mammalia.

questions.text: Which phylum does not show tissue level of organization?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phylum Nematoda, which includes roundworms, has tissue level organization. Nematodes are characterized by their long, cylindrical bodies and their lack of segmentation. They have a complete digestive system with a mouth, intestine, and anus, and a nervous system that includes a nerve ring and longitudinal nerve cords. Despite their relatively simple body plan, nematodes are a highly diverse group of animals that are found in a wide range of habitats, including soil, freshwater, and marine environments. Some species of nematodes are parasitic and can cause diseases in humans and other animals.

Phylum Cnidaria, which includes jellyfish, corals, and sea anemones, has tissue level organization. Cnidarians have a radial symmetry and specialized stinging cells called cnidocytes, which they use for defense and capturing prey. The body of a cnidarian is composed of two layers of cells: an outer layer called the ectoderm and an inner layer called the endoderm. The space between these layers is called the gastrovascular cavity, which functions in digestion and circulation of nutrients. Cnidarians are a diverse group of animals that are found in marine environments all over the world.

Phylum Porifera, which includes sponges, has no tissue level organization. Sponges are the simplest animals and are characterized by their lack of true tissues, organs, and body symmetry. Instead, they have a porous body with numerous openings called ostia, which allow water to flow through the sponge and filter out food particles.

Phylum Echinodermata, which includes sea stars, sea urchins, and sea cucumbers, has tissue level organization. Echinoderms have a radial symmetry and a water vascular system that is used for locomotion, feeding, and respiration. They also have a unique feature called a hydraulic skeleton, which allows them to change the shape and position of their body.

questions.explanation: Phylum Porifera, which includes sponges, has no tissue level organization. Sponges are the simplest animals and are characterized by their lack of true tissues, organs, and body symmetry.
